Notes: COPD: emphysema (airspace enlargement, ↓elastic recoil) + chronic bronchitis (cough ≥3mo/2yr); FEV1/FVC <0.7 post-BD

Concept: Asthma vs. COPD: asthma = reversible bronchospasm, IgE-mediated allergy, younger patients; COPD = irreversible obstruction, smoking, older; overlap (ACOS) exists.
Notes: asthma: reversible bronchospasm, IgE, younger; COPD: irreversible, smoking, older; FEV1/FVC; ACOS = overlap

Concept: The innate immune response to infection: TLR activation → NF-kB → cytokine storm (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α) → fever + inflammation + acute phase proteins; complement activation.
Notes: innate response: TLR → NF-kB → cytokines (IL-1/IL-6/TNF-α) → fever+inflammation+acute phase proteins; complement activated

Concept: Autoimmune disease mechanisms: molecular mimicry, epitope spreading, bystander activation, polyclonal activation; loss of Treg function; AIRE gene mutations cause polyendocrinopathy.
Notes: autoimmune: molecular mimicry + epitope spreading + bystander + polyclonal activation; ↓ Tregs; AIRE mutations → polyendocrinopathy

Concept: The blood coagulation cascade: intrinsic (contact activation via XII) + extrinsic (TF+VII) pathways → Xa + Va → thrombin (IIa) → fibrinogen → fibrin; PT tests extrinsic; PTT tests intrinsic.
Notes: coagulation: intrinsic (XII) + extrinsic (TF+VII) → Xa+Va → thrombin → fibrin; PT = extrinsic; PTT = intrinsic

Concept: HIV pathophysiology: RNA retrovirus → gp120 binds CD4+CCR5/CXCR4 → reverse transcriptase → proviral DNA → integrase → latent reservoir; CD4 count guides treatment.
Notes: HIV: gp120 binds CD4+CCR5/CXCR4 → reverse transcriptase → proviral DNA → integrase → latent; CD4 count guides tx

Concept: The nephron segments: PCT (reabsorb 65% Na+, glucose, AA); Loop of Henle (concentrating gradient); DCT (aldosterone-regulated Na+); collecting duct (ADH-regulated water).
Notes: nephron: PCT (65% Na+/glucose/AA reabsorption); Loop Henle (concentration gradient); DCT (aldosterone Na+); collecting duct (ADH water)

Concept: Atrial fibrillation: chaotic atrial activation → irregular ventricular response; loss of atrial kick → ↓ CO by 20-30%; thrombus in LAA → stroke risk; anticoagulate if CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2.
Notes: AFib: chaotic atria → irregular ventricles; ↓ CO 20-30%; LAA thrombus → stroke; anticoagulate if CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2

Concept: The liver's metabolic functions: gluconeogenesis + glycogenolysis (glucose homeostasis); urea cycle (NH3 → urea); bile synthesis; coagulation factors (I, II, V, VII, IX, X); albumin.
Notes: liver: gluconeogenesis+glycogenolysis; urea cycle (NH3→urea); bile synthesis; coagulation factors (I/II/V/VII/IX/X); albumin

Concept: Anemia types: iron deficiency (↓ ferritin, microcytic); B12/folate deficiency (macrocytic, megaloblastic); hemolytic (Coombs test, ↑ LDH, ↑ bilirubin); aplastic (pancytopenia).
Notes: anemia: iron deficiency (↓ferritin, microcytic); B12/folate (macrocytic/megaloblastic); hemolytic (Coombs, ↑LDH); aplastic (pancytopenia)

Concept: Pharmacodynamics: agonists activate receptor; partial agonist lower Emax; antagonist blocks receptor (competitive = reversible; non-competitive = irreversible); allosteric modulator changes affinity.
Notes: PD: agonist=activates; partial agonist=↓Emax; competitive antagonist=reversible block; non-competitive=irreversible; allosteric=changes affinity

Concept: Cancer staging: TNM system; T = primary tumor size/invasion; N = lymph node involvement; M = distant metastasis; Stage IV = any T/N + M1; guides treatment choice.
Notes: TNM staging: T=tumor size/invasion; N=lymph node; M=metastasis; Stage IV = M1; guides treatment

Concept: Immunosuppression after transplant: calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) block T cell activation; azathioprine/MMF = antimetabolites; steroids = broad immunosuppression.
Notes: transplant immunosuppression: calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine/tacrolimus) block T-cell; azathioprine/MMF = antimetabolites; steroids = broad suppression

Concept: The Glasgow Coma Scale: E (eyes, max 4) + V (verbal, max 5) + M (motor, max 6) = 3-15; ≤8 = severe TBI/intubate; 9-12 = moderate; 13-15 = mild.
Notes: GCS: E(max4)+V(max5)+M(max6)=3-15; ≤8=severe (intubate); 9-12=moderate; 13-15=mild TBI

Concept: Beta-blocker classes: cardioselective (B1-selective: metoprolol, atenolol); non-selective (propranolol, also B2); also with alpha blockade (labetalol, carvedilol); ISA property (pindolol).
Notes: beta-blockers: B1-selective=metoprolol/atenolol; non-selective=propranolol (also B2); alpha+beta=labetalol/carvedilol; ISA=pindolol

Concept: The enteric nervous system: 'second brain'; 500M neurons; ENS1 = Meissner's (submucosal, secretion) + Auerbach's plexus (myenteric, motility); largely autonomous from CNS.
Notes: ENS: 500M neurons; Meissner's plexus (submucosal, secretion) + Auerbach's/myenteric plexus (motility); autonomous from CNS

Concept: Hyperkalemia: causes = renal failure, ACEi/ARBs, K+-sparing diuretics, acidosis, hemolysis; ECG changes: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave → VF; treat: Ca2+, insulin+glucose, kayexalate.
Notes: hyperkalemia: causes=renal failure/ACEi/ARB/K+-sparing/acidosis; ECG: peaked T→wide QRS→sine wave→VF; tx: Ca2+, insulin+glucose

Concept: Corticosteroids: anti-inflammatory (block arachidonic acid via lipocortin/annexin I); immunosuppressive; side effects: Cushing syndrome, osteoporosis, hyperglycemia, adrenal suppression.
Notes: corticosteroids: anti-inflam via lipocortin (block arachidonic acid); immunosuppressive; SEs: Cushing/osteoporosis/hyperglycemia/adrenal suppression

Concept: Pneumonia pathogens by host: community-acquired (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, atypicals: Mycoplasma, Legionella); hospital-acquired (Pseudomonas, MRSA, Acinetobacter).
Notes: pneumonia: CAP = S. pneumo/H. flu/atypicals (Mycoplasma/Legionella); HAP = Pseudomonas/MRSA/Acinetobacter

Concept: The thyroid axis: TRH (hypothalamus) → TSH (pituitary) → T3/T4 (thyroid); T4 is prohormone; T3 is active (10× more potent); negative feedback on TRH + TSH; TPO synthesizes hormones.
Notes: thyroid axis: TRH → TSH → T3/T4; T4=prohormone; T3=active (10× T4); negative feedback; TPO synthesizes T3/T4

Concept: Fluid compartments: total body water = 60% body weight; ICF = 2/3 TBW; ECF = 1/3 (plasma 1/4 + interstitial 3/4 of ECF); osmolarity = 285-295 mOsm/kg.
Notes: fluid compartments: TBW=60% BW; ICF=2/3 TBW; ECF=1/3 (plasma 1/4 + interstitial 3/4 of ECF); osmolarity ~290 mOsm/kg

Concept: Acute kidney injury (AKI) causes: prerenal (↓ perfusion, ↑ BUN/Cr ratio >20); intrinsic (ATN, most common, FeNa>2%); postrenal (obstruction); KDIGO criteria: Cr ↑ 0.3mg/dL or >1.5× baseline.
Notes: AKI: prerenal (↓ perfusion, BUN/Cr>20); intrinsic/ATN (FeNa>2%); postrenal (obstruction); KDIGO: Cr↑0.3 or >1.5× baseline

Concept: Anticoagulants: heparin (activates ATIII → inhibits Xa + IIa; immediate; monitor aPTT); warfarin (inhibits VKORC1 → ↓ II/VII/IX/X; monitor INR); DOACs (direct Xa or IIa inhibitors).
Notes: anticoagulants: heparin (ATIII→Xa+IIa; immediate; monitor aPTT); warfarin (VKORC1 → ↓ II/VII/IX/X; INR); DOACs (direct Xa or IIa)

Concept: Phenomenology (Husserl): study of conscious experience from first-person perspective; bracketing (epoché) = suspend natural attitude; intentionality = consciousness always 'about' something.
Notes: phenomenology (Husserl): 1st-person conscious experience; epoché = bracket natural attitude; intentionality = consciousness always directed

Concept: Intertextuality (Kristeva): all texts absorb and transform other texts; meaning arises from relations between texts; every text is mosaic of quotations; reader co-produces meaning.
Notes: intertextuality (Kristeva): all texts = transformed other texts; meaning from text relations; text = mosaic of quotations

Concept: The linguistic turn in philosophy: Wittgenstein, Frege, Russell; philosophical problems as linguistic confusions; language shapes thought; analytic philosophy focus on clarity.
Notes: linguistic turn: Wittgenstein/Frege/Russell; philosophical problems = linguistic confusions; language shapes thought; analytic phil focus

Concept: Structuralism (Saussure): language as system of signs; sign = signifier (sound image) + signified (concept); meaning from difference, not inherent; langue (system) vs. parole (use).
Notes: structuralism (Saussure): sign = signifier + signified; meaning from difference not essence; langue (system) vs. parole (use)

Concept: Existentialism (Sartre): 'existence precedes essence' - no predetermined nature; radical freedom + responsibility; bad faith = self-deception about freedom; condemned to be free.
Notes: existentialism (Sartre): existence precedes essence; radical freedom + responsibility; bad faith = denying freedom; 'condemned to be free'

Concept: Narrative theory: fabula (chronological story events) vs. syuzhet (narrative arrangement); focalization (narrative perspective: zero, internal, external); unreliable narrator.
Notes: narrative theory: fabula (events chronological) vs. syuzhet (narrative arrangement); focalization: zero/internal/external; unreliable narrator

Concept: Marxist literary criticism: literature reflects ideology of ruling class; base (economic relations) determines superstructure (culture, law, art); Althusser: ideology as lived practice.
Notes: Marxist lit crit: literature = ruling class ideology; base (economy) → superstructure (culture/law/art); Althusser: ideology = lived practice

Concept: Post-structuralism (Derrida): deconstruction reveals internal contradictions in texts; différance = meaning always deferred; logocentrism = privilege of presence over absence.
Notes: post-structuralism (Derrida): deconstruction = expose text contradictions; différance = meaning deferred; logocentrism = privilege presence

Concept: The unreliable narrator: narrator whose credibility is compromised by limited knowledge, psychological instability, or vested interest; reader must infer 'true' story; Stevens (Remains of the Day).
Notes: unreliable narrator: compromised by limited knowledge/instability/interest; reader infers true story; e.g., Stevens (Remains of the Day)

Concept: Speech act theory (Austin, Searle): utterances perform actions; locutionary (what is said) + illocutionary (what is done, e.g., promise, warn) + perlocutionary (effect on hearer).
Notes: speech acts (Austin/Searle): locutionary (what said) + illocutionary (what done: promise/warn) + perlocutionary (effect on hearer)

Concept: Chomsky's universal grammar: language acquisition device innate in humans; poverty of the stimulus argument: children acquire grammar with insufficient input; parameters vary across languages.
Notes: Chomsky UG: innate LAD; poverty of stimulus = children learn grammar w/ insufficient input; parameters vary across languages

Concept: The sapir-whorf hypothesis and color terms: languages differ in color vocabulary; weak version: language affects color perception at category boundaries; confirmed by Berlin-Kay universals.
Notes: Sapir-Whorf + color: language affects perception at category boundaries; Berlin-Kay universals: 11 basic color terms; weak version confirmed

Concept: Tragedy (Aristotle's Poetics): imitation of serious action; hamartia = tragic flaw; catharsis = emotional purging; peripeteia (reversal) + anagnorisis (recognition).
Notes: tragedy (Aristotle): imitation of serious action; hamartia = tragic flaw; catharsis = purging; peripeteia (reversal) + anagnorisis (recognition)

Concept: The hermeneutic circle: understanding a text requires understanding parts; understanding parts requires understanding the whole; interpretation = iterative process.
Notes: hermeneutic circle: whole → parts → whole → ...; understanding requires iterative interpretation; cannot fully escape

Concept: Magic realism: realistic narrative with magical elements presented matter-of-factly; Garcia Marquez, Borges; Latin American context; challenges colonial epistemology.
Notes: magic realism: realistic narrative + matter-of-fact magic; Garcia Marquez/Borges; challenges colonial epistemology

Concept: The Frankfurt School: critical theory; alienation under capitalism; culture industry manufactures consent (Adorno/Horkheimer); Habermas: communicative rationality vs. strategic.
Notes: Frankfurt School: critical theory; culture industry manufactures consent (Adorno/Horkheimer); Habermas: communicative rationality ≠ strategic

Concept: Cognitive poetics: applies cognitive science to literature; conceptual metaphor (Lakoff-Johnson) structures thought; schema theory; narrative comprehension as mental simulation.
Notes: cognitive poetics: cog sci → literature; conceptual metaphor (Lakoff-Johnson) structures thought; schema theory; narrative = mental simulation

Concept: The postcolonial uncanny: colonial past haunts present; Bhabha's mimicry = colonial subject imitates colonizer but never fully; hybridity; ambivalence.
Notes: postcolonial uncanny: colonial past haunts; Bhabha mimicry = colonial imitation that never fully succeeds; hybridity + ambivalence

Concept: Pragmatics vs. semantics: semantics = literal meaning of words/sentences; pragmatics = meaning in context; Grice's maxims (quantity, quality, relation, manner) govern conversation.
Notes: semantics = literal meaning; pragmatics = contextual meaning; Grice maxims: quantity+quality+relation+manner govern conversation

Concept: The picaresque novel: episodic narrative of low-born protagonist navigating corrupt society; social satire; Lazarillo de Tormes; ironic first-person narrator.
Notes: picaresque: episodic + low-born protagonist + corrupt society; social satire; Lazarillo de Tormes; ironic 1st-person

Concept: The gaze (Lacan, Mulvey): cinema's male gaze objectifies female; scopophilia; gaze as power; Lacan: gaze as objet petit a; subverted by counter-cinema.
Notes: male gaze (Mulvey/Lacan): cinema objectifies female; scopophilia; gaze=power (objet a); counter-cinema subverts

Concept: The sublime (Kant, Burke): experience of overwhelming vastness or power; mathematical sublime (size) vs. dynamic (power); transforms terror into aesthetic pleasure; mountain, storm.
Notes: sublime: Burke (terror→pleasure); Kant: mathematical (size) + dynamic (power); transforms terror into aesthetic experience

Concept: Feminist literary criticism: first wave = equal access; second wave (70s) = gender in representation; third wave = intersectionality; Showalter: gynocriticism (women's tradition).
Notes: feminist lit crit: 1st wave (access); 2nd wave (gender representation); 3rd wave (intersectionality); Showalter gynocriticism = women's tradition

Concept: The Oulipo movement: literature under formal constraints; Perec's La Disparition (novel without letter 'e'); Queneau's 100,000 billion poems; constraint as generative device.
Notes: Oulipo: literature under formal constraints; Perec (novel w/o 'e'); Queneau (100T billion poems); constraint = generative device

Concept: Deontological ethics (Kant): moral worth from motive, not consequences; duty-based; categorical imperative: act only on maxims you could universalize; persons as ends, not means.
Notes: deontology (Kant): moral worth from duty/motive; categorical imperative: universalizable maxim; persons as ends never merely means

Concept: Consequentialism vs. virtue ethics: consequentialist = judge acts by outcomes; utilitarian calculus; virtue ethics (Aristotle): cultivate virtues (courage, justice, temperance); eudaimonia = flourishing.
Notes: consequentialism: acts judged by outcomes; utilitarian calculus; virtue ethics (Aristotle): cultivate virtues → eudaimonia (flourishing)

Concept: The free rider problem in ethics: public goods provision hindered when rational agents benefit without contributing; tragedy of commons; collective action problem.
Notes: free rider: rational agents benefit w/o contributing; public goods underproduced; tragedy of commons = collective action failure

Concept: Aesthetic formalism: artworks judged solely by formal properties (line, color, composition, harmony) not content or context; Bell's significant form; Hanslick on music.
Notes: formalism: judge art by formal properties only (line/color/composition); content/context irrelevant; Bell's significant form; Hanslick on music

Concept: Language and thought (Whorf): language shapes the categories available for thought; debated; experiments on color perception support weak version; strong determinism rejected.
Notes: Whorf: language shapes thought categories; strong determinism rejected; weak influence = supported (e.g., color perception studies)

Concept: The problem of other minds: cannot directly access another's consciousness; argument from analogy (I have pain, they behave like me, ∴ they have pain); solipsism risk.
Notes: other minds problem: cannot access another's consciousness; analogy argument: behavior similar → infer inner states; solipsism risk

Concept: Stream of consciousness technique: narrative mirrors unfiltered flow of thoughts; Virginia Woolf, James Joyce, William Faulkner; interiority + associative logic; challenges linear narrative.
Notes: stream of consciousness: unfiltered thought flow; Woolf/Joyce/Faulkner; interiority + association; challenges linear narrative

Concept: Postmodernism in literature: fragmentation, metafiction (narrative about narrative), intertextuality, irony, distrust of grand narratives; Lyotard: 'incredulity toward metanarratives.'
Notes: postmodern lit: fragmentation + metafiction + intertextuality + irony; Lyotard: incredulity toward metanarratives; distrust of grand narratives

Concept: The canon debate: Western literary canon = exclusion of women, non-Western, marginalized voices; multiculturalism vs. universalism; Bloom's anxiety of influence; ongoing tension.
Notes: canon debate: exclusion of women/non-Western/marginalized; multiculturalism vs. universalism; Bloom = anxiety of influence

Concept: Moral relativism vs. moral realism: relativism = moral truths relative to culture/individual; no universal standards; realism = objective moral facts exist independent of opinion.
Notes: moral relativism: moral truths = culture/context-dependent; ≠ universals; moral realism: objective moral facts exist regardless of opinion

Concept: The trolley problem (Foot, Thomson): kill 1 to save 5 (utilitarian intuition) vs. doing vs. allowing harm; doctrine of double effect; tests deontological limits.
Notes: trolley problem: kill 1 to save 5; utilitarian (kill 1) vs. deontological (doing harm); doctrine of double effect; tests moral intuitions

Concept: Intersectionality in literary criticism: texts read through multiple identity axes simultaneously; Crenshaw + hooks + Anzaldúa; identity categories interact, not additive.
Notes: intersectionality in lit: read through multiple identity axes; Crenshaw+hooks+Anzaldúa; axes interact (not additive)

Concept: The narrator vs. implied author: implied author = image of author constructed from text; narrator = text's voice (may differ from author); narratee = text's imagined audience.
Notes: narrator ≠ implied author; implied author = textual image of author; narratee = imagined audience; Wayne Booth's distinction

Concept: Wittgenstein's language games: meaning = use; different language games have different rules; family resemblance (no single essence to concepts); private language argument against solipsism.
Notes: Wittgenstein: meaning = use; language games w/ different rules; family resemblance (no essence); private language = impossible → anti-solipsism

Concept: The hermeneutics of suspicion (Ricoeur): read texts against grain; Marx (ideology), Nietzsche (power), Freud (unconscious) as 'masters of suspicion'; meaning hidden beneath surface.
Notes: hermeneutics of suspicion (Ricoeur): read against grain; Marx (ideology) + Nietzsche (power) + Freud (unconscious) = masters of suspicion

Concept: Narrative identity (Ricoeur): self constructed through narrative; idem (sameness over time) vs. ipse (self-constancy in promise); life as story we tell about ourselves.
Notes: narrative identity (Ricoeur): self = narrative construction; idem (sameness) vs. ipse (constancy in promises); life = self-authored story

Concept: Ecocriticism: literature + natural environment; how nature is represented; anthropocentrism critiqued; Lawrence Buell; Anthropocene literature; place as character.
Notes: ecocriticism: literature + environment; how nature represented; critiques anthropocentrism; Anthropocene lit; place as character

Concept: Reader-response theory: meaning co-created by reader + text; Fish's interpretive communities; Iser's implied reader (blanks to fill); Jauss's horizon of expectations.
Notes: reader-response: meaning = reader+text; Fish = interpretive communities; Iser = implied reader fills blanks; Jauss = horizon of expectations

Concept: The philosophy of time: presentism (only present exists); eternalism (past/present/future equally real, B-theory); growing block (past+present real, future not); McTaggart A/B series.
Notes: philosophy of time: presentism (only present); eternalism (all times real, B-theory); growing block (past+present); McTaggart A/B series

Concept: Bioethics principles (Beauchamp-Childress): autonomy (respect patient choice) + beneficence (do good) + non-maleficence (avoid harm) + justice (fair distribution); principlism.
Notes: bioethics (Beauchamp-Childress): autonomy + beneficence + non-maleficence + justice; principlism; four principles balance in clinical decisions

Concept: When oxygen levels drop in tissues, hypoxia-inducible factor 1-alpha stabilizes and enters the nucleus to activate genes for erythropoiesis and angiogenesis.
Notes: hypoxia → HIF-1α stable → nucleus → ↑ erythropoiesis + ↑ angiogenesis genes

Concept: Increasing insulin concentration causes glucose transporters to migrate to the cell membrane, increasing glucose uptake and lowering blood glucose.
Notes: ↑ insulin → GLUT4 → membrane → ↑ glucose uptake → ↓ blood glucose

Concept: When epinephrine binds beta-adrenergic receptors, cAMP rises, activating PKA, which phosphorylates glycogen phosphorylase to release glucose.
Notes: epinephrine → β-receptor → ↑ cAMP → PKA active → glycogen phosphorylase-P → ↑ glucose

Concept: Loss of PTEN function removes inhibition of the PI3K pathway, causing constitutive AKT activation and promoting cell survival and proliferation.
Notes: PTEN loss → PI3K uninhibited → ↑ AKT → ↑ cell survival + ↑ proliferation; ∴ tumorigenic

Concept: Interferon-gamma released by T-helper 1 cells activates macrophages to increase production of reactive oxygen species, enhancing killing of intracellular pathogens.
Notes: IFN-γ (Th1) → macrophage activation → ↑ ROS → ↑ intracellular pathogen killing

Concept: As tumor cells secrete VEGF into the local environment, nearby endothelial cells proliferate and form new blood vessels to supply the growing tumor.
Notes: tumor → ↑ VEGF → endothelial cell proliferation → neovascularization → ↑ tumor supply

Concept: Glucocorticoid excess suppresses the immune response by inhibiting NF-kB transcription factor, reducing production of pro-inflammatory cytokines.
Notes: ↑ glucocorticoids → inhibit NF-κB → ↓ pro-inflammatory cytokines → ↓ immune response

Concept: The absence of ligand on the Notch receptor leads to degradation of the intracellular domain, preventing target gene activation and maintaining stem cell quiescence.
Notes: Notch w/o ligand → NICD degraded → no target gene activation → stem cell quiescence maintained

Concept: When estrogen levels fall after menopause, osteoclast activity exceeds osteoblast activity, leading to net bone loss and increased fracture risk.
Notes: ↓ estrogen (menopause) → osteoclast > osteoblast → net bone loss → ↑ fracture risk

Concept: Activation of Toll-like receptor 4 by lipopolysaccharide triggers the MyD88 adaptor pathway, activating NF-kB and inducing expression of inflammatory genes.
Notes: LPS → TLR4 → MyD88 → NF-κB active → ↑ inflammatory gene expression

Concept: High glucose concentrations in diabetic patients drive reactive oxygen species production in mitochondria, damaging endothelial cells and causing vascular complications.
Notes: ↑ glucose (diabetes) → ↑ mitochondrial ROS → endothelial damage → vascular complications

Concept: Depletion of intracellular NAD+ slows the TCA cycle and electron transport chain, reducing ATP production and impairing cellular metabolism.
Notes: ↓ NAD+ → ↓ TCA cycle + ↓ ETC → ↓ ATP → impaired metabolism

Concept: When DNA damage accumulates beyond repair capacity, p53 shifts from activating cell cycle arrest genes to activating pro-apoptotic genes like BAX.
Notes: severe DNA damage → p53 → BAX ↑ (pro-apoptotic) ≠ p21 ↑ (arrest); ∴ apoptosis not repair

Concept: Increased acetyl-CoA from fatty acid oxidation inhibits pyruvate dehydrogenase, blocking conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA from glucose.
Notes: ↑ acetyl-CoA (from β-oxidation) → inhibits PDH → ↓ pyruvate → acetyl-CoA from glucose; glucose oxidation ↓

Concept: When calcium concentration rises in the cytoplasm, calmodulin binds calcium, changes shape, and activates downstream kinases including CaM kinase II.
Notes: ↑ cytoplasmic Ca²⁺ → Ca²⁺ binds calmodulin → conformational change → CaM-kinase II active

Concept: Phosphorylation of the retinoblastoma protein by cyclin D-CDK4/6 releases E2F transcription factor, allowing progression through the G1/S checkpoint.
Notes: cyclin D-CDK4/6 → Rb-P → E2F released → G1/S checkpoint passed → S phase entry

Concept: Reactive nitrogen species produced by iNOS in macrophages kill bacteria by reacting with ROS to form peroxynitrite, which damages microbial DNA and proteins.
Notes: iNOS (macrophage) → NO• + ROS → ONOO⁻ (peroxynitrite) → microbial DNA/protein damage → killing

Concept: As a cell grows larger, the surface area to volume ratio decreases, limiting nutrient diffusion and waste removal, triggering division.
Notes: ↑ cell size → ↓ SA:V ratio → ↓ diffusion efficiency → cell division triggered

Concept: Increased production of short-chain fatty acids by gut microbiota strengthens tight junctions in the gut epithelium, reducing intestinal permeability.
Notes: ↑ gut microbiota SCFA → ↑ tight junctions → ↓ intestinal permeability; gut barrier ↑

Concept: When mTOR is inhibited by nutrient deprivation, ULK1 kinase is activated, initiating the autophagy cascade and promoting degradation of cellular components.
Notes: nutrient deprivation → ↓ mTOR → ULK1 active → autophagy initiated → ↑ cellular component degradation

Concept: Gain-of-function mutations in KRAS lock the protein in the GTP-bound active state, constitutively activating downstream proliferation pathways regardless of growth factor input.
Notes: KRAS gain-of-function → KRAS-GTP locked → constitutive MAPK/PI3K → ↑ proliferation w/o GF input

Concept: High levels of homocysteine deplete tetrahydrofolate, impairing thymidylate synthesis, which reduces dTTP for DNA replication and causes megaloblastic changes.
Notes: ↑ homocysteine → ↓ THF → ↓ thymidylate synthesis → ↓ dTTP → ↓ DNA replication → megaloblastic changes

Concept: Activation of the cGAS-STING pathway by cytoplasmic DNA produces type I interferons that restrict viral replication and activate innate immune defenses.
Notes: cytoplasmic DNA → cGAS → cGAMP → STING → ↑ type I IFN → ↓ viral replication + ↑ innate immunity

Concept: Prolactin released after birth inhibits GnRH secretion, suppressing ovulation and providing natural postpartum contraception while breastfeeding.
Notes: breastfeeding → ↑ prolactin → ↓ GnRH → ↓ LH/FSH → ↓ ovulation; ∴ natural postpartum contraception

Concept: Sickle-cell trait provides a selective advantage in malaria-endemic regions because red cells with HbS sickle under low oxygen, killing the Plasmodium parasite.
Notes: HbS trait + malaria region → ↓ O₂ → RBC sickle → Plasmodium killed → ↑ survival; balanced polymorphism

Concept: Long-term potentiation requires AMPA receptor insertion into the postsynaptic membrane after NMDA receptor activation, increasing synaptic strength.
Notes: NMDA activated → AMPA receptors → synaptic membrane insertion → ↑ synaptic strength → LTP

Concept: High-fat diets increase ceramide production, which impairs insulin signaling by activating PP2A to dephosphorylate AKT, causing insulin resistance.
Notes: ↑ fat → ↑ ceramide → PP2A active → AKT-P removed → AKT inactive → insulin resistance

Concept: Release of ATP from damaged cells activates purinergic receptors on immune cells, triggering the NLRP3 inflammasome and IL-1beta release.
Notes: cell damage → ATP released → P2X7 receptor → NLRP3 inflammasome → ↑ IL-1β; danger signaling

Concept: As aerobic fitness increases, mitochondrial biogenesis is stimulated in muscle cells, increasing oxidative capacity and reducing reliance on anaerobic glycolysis.
Notes: ↑ aerobic fitness → ↑ mitochondrial biogenesis → ↑ oxidative capacity → ↓ anaerobic glycolysis reliance

Concept: Loss of E-cadherin expression in epithelial cells disrupts cell-cell adhesion and activates beta-catenin signaling, contributing to epithelial-mesenchymal transition.
Notes: ↓ E-cadherin → ↓ cell-cell adhesion + ↑ β-catenin signaling → EMT → ↑ invasiveness

Concept: Telomere shortening activates ATM kinase, which phosphorylates p53, leading to cell cycle arrest or apoptosis depending on the degree of damage.
Notes: telomere shortening → ATM activated → p53-P → cell cycle arrest or apoptosis; ∝ damage severity

Concept: Overexpression of anti-apoptotic BCL-2 family proteins prevents cytochrome c release from mitochondria, allowing cells to evade intrinsic apoptosis.
Notes: ↑ BCL-2 → blocks cytochrome c release from mito → intrinsic apoptosis evaded → ↑ cell survival

Concept: Increased reactive oxygen species damage the electron transport chain, reducing membrane potential, which further increases ROS in a positive feedback loop.
Notes: ↑ ROS → ETC damage → ↓ membrane potential → ↑ ROS; positive feedback loop; mitochondrial dysfunction

Concept: Glucagon binds its receptor in liver cells, raising cAMP, activating PKA, which phosphorylates PFK-2 to lower fructose-2,6-bisphosphate, inhibiting glycolysis.
Notes: glucagon → ↑ cAMP → PKA → PFK-2-P → ↓ F-2,6-BP → ↓ glycolysis; ∴ ↑ gluconeogenesis

Concept: Elevated ammonia from protein catabolism is toxic to the brain; the urea cycle converts it to urea for excretion, requiring bicarbonate and aspartate.
Notes: ↑ NH₃ (protein catabolism) → toxic to brain; urea cycle → NH₃ + HCO₃⁻ + aspartate → urea → excretion

Concept: Upon B cell activation by antigen, the germinal center reaction somatic hypermutation and class switch recombination increase antibody diversity and affinity.
Notes: antigen → B cell activation → germinal center → somatic hypermutation + CSR → ↑ Ab diversity + ↑ affinity

Concept: Increased sympathetic nervous system activity raises heart rate by decreasing the time for depolarization in the sinoatrial node pacemaker cells.
Notes: ↑ sympathetic → ↓ SA node depolarization interval → ↑ HR; norepinephrine + β1-adrenergic receptor

Concept: Deletion of a mismatch repair gene such as MLH1 allows replication errors to accumulate, dramatically increasing mutation rate and cancer risk.
Notes: MLH1 deletion → ↓ mismatch repair → ↑ replication errors → ↑ mutation rate → ↑ cancer risk

Concept: As the partial pressure of CO2 rises in blood, more CO2 dissolves, forming carbonic acid, which dissociates to lower pH, triggering increased breathing rate.
Notes: ↑ pCO₂ → CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ → ↓ pH → ↑ ventilation rate; chemoreceptor reflex

Concept: Nitric oxide produced by endothelial cells diffuses into smooth muscle cells, activates guanylyl cyclase, raising cGMP, causing smooth muscle relaxation and vasodilation.
Notes: endothelial cells → NO → smooth muscle → ↑ cGMP (guanylyl cyclase) → smooth muscle relaxation → vasodilation

Concept: Absence of dystrophin disrupts the link between the cytoskeleton and extracellular matrix, causing membrane fragility and progressive muscle fiber degeneration.
Notes: dystrophin absent → cytoskeleton ↔ ECM link lost → membrane fragility → progressive muscle degeneration (DMD)

Concept: During exercise, rising AMP levels activate AMPK, which stimulates glucose uptake, fatty acid oxidation, and mitochondrial biogenesis while inhibiting anabolic pathways.
Notes: exercise → ↑ AMP → AMPK active → ↑ glucose uptake + ↑ β-oxidation + ↑ mito biogenesis; ↓ anabolism

Concept: Renal proximal tubule cells reabsorb filtered bicarbonate by converting it to CO2, which diffuses in and is reconverted to bicarbonate inside the cell.
Notes: PCT: filtered HCO₃⁻ → CO₂ (at brush border via CA) → into cell → CO₂ + H₂O → HCO₃⁻ (CA) → blood; HCO₃⁻ reabsorbed

Concept: High aldosterone levels stimulate sodium reabsorption in the collecting duct, drawing water osmotically, raising blood volume and increasing blood pressure.
Notes: ↑ aldosterone → ↑ Na⁺ reabsorption (collecting duct) → osmotic water follows → ↑ blood volume → ↑ BP

Concept: When GnRH is released in a pulsatile manner it stimulates LH and FSH release; continuous GnRH exposure paradoxically desensitizes pituitary receptors and reduces gonadotropins.
Notes: GnRH pulsatile → ↑ LH + FSH; GnRH continuous → receptor desensitization → ↓ LH + FSH; ∴ therapeutic target

Concept: Complement protein C3b deposited on pathogens acts as an opsonin, binding complement receptors on macrophages and dramatically increasing phagocytosis efficiency.
Notes: C3b on pathogen → opsonin → CR3 on macrophage → ↑ phagocytosis efficiency; complement opsonization

Concept: High concentrations of substrate inhibit the enzyme aspartate transcarbamoylase through end-product inhibition by CTP, reducing pyrimidine biosynthesis.
Notes: ↑ CTP → allosteric inhibition of ATCase → ↓ pyrimidine biosynthesis; negative feedback end-product inhibition

Concept: Deficiency of vitamin C impairs hydroxylation of proline and lysine in procollagen, preventing proper triple helix formation and causing scurvy.
Notes: ↓ vitamin C → ↓ prolyl/lysyl hydroxylation → collagen triple helix ↓ → scurvy; ∵ ascorbate = cofactor for hydroxylase

Concept: Elevation of intracellular calcium in cardiac myocytes through the L-type channel triggers calcium-induced calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptors.
Notes: ↑ Ca²⁺ via L-type channel → CICR: ryanodine receptor (SR) → ↑ Ca²⁺ → contraction; cardiac E-C coupling

Concept: PD-L1 expressed on tumor cells binds PD-1 on T cells, sending an inhibitory signal that reduces T cell activation and allows the tumor to evade immune surveillance.
Notes: tumor PD-L1 → T cell PD-1 → inhibitory signal → ↓ T cell activation → tumor immune evasion; ∴ anti-PD-1 = therapy

Concept: Decreased dietary protein intake lowers branched-chain amino acid levels in the blood, reducing mTORC1 activation and shifting cells toward autophagy.
Notes: ↓ dietary protein → ↓ BCAA → ↓ mTORC1 → autophagy ↑; catabolism > anabolism; starvation response

Concept: When temperature increases in a gas at constant volume, the pressure rises proportionally because molecules collide more frequently and with greater force.
Notes: ↑ T (const V) → ↑ molecular velocity → ↑ collision freq + force → ↑ P; P ∝ T (Gay-Lussac)

Concept: Adding a catalyst to a reaction lowers the activation energy, increasing the rate without changing the equilibrium position or thermodynamic favorability.
Notes: catalyst → ↓ Eₐ → ↑ rxn rate; equilibrium position unchanged; ΔG unchanged; ≠ thermodynamic change

Concept: As the atomic number increases across a period, the effective nuclear charge increases while electron shielding remains approximately constant, drawing electrons closer and shrinking atomic radius.
Notes: ↑ Z (across period) → ↑ effective nuclear charge; shielding ≈ const → ↓ atomic radius

Concept: Increasing the concentration of a weak acid solution does not proportionally increase H+ concentration because the dissociation equilibrium shifts but Ka remains fixed.
Notes: ↑ weak acid conc → ↑ [H⁺] but not proportionally; Ka fixed; ∴ % dissociation ↓ as conc ↑

Concept: When a reaction reaches equilibrium, adding more reactant shifts the equilibrium to the right, increasing product concentration until a new equilibrium is established.
Notes: add reactant → Q < K → equilibrium shifts right → ↑ product until new eq; Le Chatelier

Concept: Increasing pressure on a gas-phase equilibrium with unequal moles of gas on each side shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer moles of gas.
Notes: ↑ P → equilibrium shifts toward fewer gas moles; ↓ P → shifts toward more gas moles; Le Chatelier

Concept: Strong acids completely dissociate in water, so doubling concentration doubles H+ concentration exactly, whereas weak acids show diminishing returns due to equilibrium.
Notes: strong acid: [conc] ×2 → [H⁺] ×2 exactly; weak acid: [conc] ×2 → [H⁺] <×2; ∵ Ka fixed ≠ 100% diss.

Concept: As molecular weight increases in a homologous series of alkanes, boiling point rises because London dispersion forces become stronger with larger electron clouds.
Notes: ↑ MW (alkanes) → ↑ London dispersion forces → ↑ BP; ∝ surface area + electron polarizability

Concept: Adding a non-volatile solute to a solvent raises its boiling point because the vapor pressure is lowered, requiring a higher temperature to reach atmospheric pressure.
Notes: non-volatile solute → ↓ vapor pressure → must heat more to reach P_atm → ↑ BP; ΔTb = Kb·m·i

Concept: When a photon of sufficient energy strikes a semiconductor, it promotes an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, creating an electron-hole pair.
Notes: photon (E ≥ Eg) → e⁻: valence band → conduction band → electron-hole pair; photovoltaic effect

Concept: Increasing the distance between two charges decreases the electrostatic force by the square of the distance, following Coulomb's inverse square law.
Notes: ↑ distance r → ↓ F electrostatic ∝ 1/r²; Coulomb: F=kq₁q₂/r²

Concept: In a series circuit, increasing one resistor's value increases total resistance, decreasing total current, which in turn decreases voltage across all other resistors.
Notes: series: ↑ one R → ↑ R_total → ↓ I (Ohm's) → ↓ V across all other resistors; I same throughout

Concept: In a parallel circuit, adding more resistors decreases total resistance, increasing total current drawn from the source while current through each branch is unchanged.
Notes: parallel: ↑ resistors → ↓ R_total → ↑ total I; current per branch unchanged; ≠ series behavior

Concept: As a star depletes its hydrogen fuel, it expands to become a red giant because decreased core pressure causes the outer layers to cool and expand.
Notes: ↓ H fuel in star → ↓ radiation pressure → core contracts → ↑ temp → outer layers expand + cool → red giant

Concept: When a body oscillating in simple harmonic motion is displaced further from equilibrium, the restoring force increases proportionally, but the period remains independent of amplitude.
Notes: SHM: ↑ displacement → ↑ restoring force (F=-kx); period T=2π√(m/k) independent of amplitude; ≠ pendulum at large angles

Concept: Decreasing temperature in a superconductor causes electrical resistance to drop abruptly to zero at Tc, allowing current to flow without energy dissipation.
Notes: ↓ T → Tc → resistance = 0 suddenly; current flows w/o energy loss; Cooper pairs ↔ phonons; Meissner effect

Concept: Increasing the magnetic field through a conducting loop induces an electromotive force that drives a current opposing the change, as described by Lenz's law.
Notes: ↑ B through loop → induced EMF → induced I opposes ↑ B; Lenz's law; ∵ Faraday: EMF=-dΦ/dt

Concept: When two waves of slightly different frequencies overlap, they create beats: regions of constructive and destructive interference at a rate equal to the difference in frequencies.
Notes: two waves: Δf → beats; beat freq = |f₁-f₂|; constructive ↔ destructive interference; ↓ Δf → slower beats

Concept: As hydrostatic pressure increases with depth in a fluid, buoyancy force on a submerged object equals the weight of fluid displaced, independent of depth.
Notes: ↑ depth → ↑ hydrostatic pressure; buoyancy F_b = ρ_fluid × V_submerged × g; depth independent; Archimedes

Concept: Removing electrons from an atom (ionization) requires energy; each successive electron is harder to remove because effective nuclear charge increases and shielding decreases.
Notes: successive ionization: each e⁻ harder to remove; ↑ effective nuclear charge + ↓ shielding; IE₁ < IE₂ < IE₃...

Concept: When a nuclear decay emits an alpha particle, the parent nucleus loses 2 protons and 2 neutrons, shifting two places left on the periodic table.
Notes: α decay: parent → ↓ 2 protons + ↓ 2 neutrons; new element 2 places left on periodic table; A↓4, Z↓2

Concept: Increasing the frequency of light incident on a photoelectric material increases the kinetic energy of emitted electrons but does not change the number emitted.
Notes: ↑ photon frequency → ↑ KE of emitted e⁻ (KE=hf-Φ); ≠ ↑ number of e⁻ emitted; intensity controls count

Concept: When electrons transition to lower energy levels in hydrogen, photons are emitted at discrete wavelengths corresponding to the energy difference, forming the line spectrum.
Notes: e⁻: high E level → low E level → photon emitted; E_photon = ΔE = hf; discrete → line spectrum; Bohr

Concept: As pressure increases on water ice, the melting point decreases slightly because ice is less dense than liquid water, and high pressure favors the denser liquid phase.
Notes: ↑ P on ice → ↓ melting point; ∵ liquid water denser than ice; ∴ pressure favors liquid; anomalous behavior

Concept: Entropy increases spontaneously in isolated systems; mixing two gases increases entropy because the number of possible microstates increases dramatically.
Notes: mixing gases (isolated) → ↑ microstates → ↑ entropy (ΔS > 0); spontaneous; ΔS_mix = -nR(x₁lnx₁+x₂lnx₂)

Concept: Increasing temperature in an endothermic reaction shifts equilibrium toward products, raising K, while the same temperature increase in an exothermic reaction shifts equilibrium toward reactants.
Notes: ↑ T: endothermic rxn → ↑ K (→ products); exothermic rxn → ↓ K (→ reactants); van't Hoff equation

Concept: When electrons are shared unequally between atoms of different electronegativity, the bond has partial charges, creating a dipole moment that affects molecular interactions.
Notes: unequal e⁻ sharing → partial charges (δ+/δ-) → bond dipole; ↑ Δ electronegativity → ↑ dipole moment

Concept: Adding a conjugated system of alternating single and double bonds lowers the energy gap between ground and excited states, shifting absorption to longer wavelengths.
Notes: ↑ conjugation length → ↓ HOMO-LUMO gap → ↑ absorption wavelength (bathochromic shift); π→π* transition

Concept: When a crystal is placed under tensile stress, planes of atoms slip relative to each other at dislocations, allowing plastic deformation at stresses far below theoretical strength.
Notes: tensile stress → dislocation slip → plastic deformation; actual strength << theoretical; ∵ dislocation motion requires ↓ force

Concept: As the chain length of a polymer increases, its glass transition temperature rises because longer chains have fewer chain ends and reduced free volume.
Notes: ↑ polymer chain length → ↓ chain ends → ↓ free volume → ↑ Tg; more restricted chain mobility

Concept: Decreasing the distance between capacitor plates at constant charge increases the electric field and raises the voltage, increasing the stored energy.
Notes: ↓ plate distance (const Q) → ↑ E field → ↑ V → ↑ stored energy (U=Q²/2C); C=ε₀A/d so ↓d → ↓C → ↑V

Concept: When a radioactive isotope decays, the remaining activity decreases exponentially; after each half-life exactly half the nuclei remain undecayed.
Notes: radioactive decay: A(t)=A₀e^(-λt); after t½: 50% remain; t½=ln2/λ=0.693/λ; exponential ↓ always

Concept: Increasing the number of diffraction grating lines per millimeter narrows and brightens diffraction maxima, increasing resolving power and ability to distinguish closely spaced wavelengths.
Notes: ↑ grating lines/mm → narrower + brighter maxima → ↑ resolving power R=mN → ↑ wavelength resolution

Concept: As the electron configuration fills from lower to higher energy subshells, atoms with half-filled or fully-filled subshells gain extra stability due to exchange energy.
Notes: half-filled (d⁵) or full (d¹⁰) subshells → ↑ stability via exchange energy; Cr=[Ar]3d⁵4s¹ ≠ predicted [Ar]3d⁴4s²

Concept: When acid is added to a buffer, the conjugate base neutralizes the H+ ions, resisting large pH changes; the buffer capacity is greatest when pH equals pKa.
Notes: add H⁺ to buffer → A⁻ + H⁺ → HA; ↓ pH change; max buffer capacity at pH = pKa; [A⁻]=[HA]

Concept: Decreasing the pH of a solution increases the concentration of hydrogen ions, shifting protein equilibria and potentially denaturing enzymes at extreme values.
Notes: ↓ pH → ↑ [H⁺] → protein protonation shifts → altered folding; extreme pH → denaturation; enzyme activity ↓

Concept: When both reactant concentrations double in a second-order reaction, the rate increases by a factor of four, consistent with rate equals k times concentration squared.
Notes: 2nd order rxn: [A]×2 → rate ×4; rate=k[A]²; ∵ (2)²=4; ≠ 1st order where [A]×2 → rate ×2

Concept: Increasing temperature increases the fraction of molecules with energy exceeding the activation energy, exponentially increasing the reaction rate as described by the Arrhenius equation.
Notes: ↑ T → ↑ fraction of molecules with E>Eₐ → ↑ rxn rate exponentially; k=Ae^(-Eₐ/RT)

Concept: In an electrochemical cell, the species with the higher standard reduction potential is reduced at the cathode, and the cell operates spontaneously when E°cell is positive.
Notes: higher E° → cathode (reduction); lower E° → anode (oxidation); spontaneous if E°cell > 0; ΔG°=-nFE°

Concept: When the temperature of a gas decreases at constant pressure, the volume decreases proportionally because molecules slow down and exert less pressure per unit area.
Notes: ↓ T (const P) → ↓ molecular velocity → ↓ volume proportionally; V ∝ T (Charles's law); V=nRT/P

Concept: Excited electrons in a laser medium return to lower energy states and emit photons that stimulate further emission of identical photons, causing coherent light amplification.
Notes: excited e⁻ → stimulated emission → identical photons → coherent amplification; LASER: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission

Concept: As the ionization energy of an element increases, it becomes harder to form cations, making the element a poorer reducing agent and a better oxidizing agent.
Notes: ↑ IE → harder to form cations → poorer reducing agent → ↑ oxidizing power; IE ↔ oxidizing ability

Concept: When light passes from a denser to a less dense medium at an angle greater than the critical angle, total internal reflection occurs and no refracted ray escapes.
Notes: dense → less dense medium; θ > θ_c → TIR; no refracted ray; θ_c = arcsin(n₂/n₁); ∵ n₁>n₂

Concept: Increasing pressure on a system at equilibrium involving gases shifts the equilibrium toward the side with fewer total moles of gas to reduce pressure.
Notes: ↑ P → equilibrium → side w/ fewer gas moles; ↓ P → side w/ more gas moles; only affects gas-phase rxns

Concept: Lowering the temperature of liquid water below its freezing point causes hydrogen bonds to organize into a hexagonal lattice, forming ice less dense than liquid.
Notes: ↓ T below 0°C → H-bonds → hexagonal lattice → ice; ice ↓ density vs liquid; ∴ floats; anomalous

Concept: When a buffer solution is diluted, the ratio of conjugate base to acid remains constant, so the pH does not change significantly despite volume increase.
Notes: dilute buffer → [A⁻]/[HA] ratio unchanged → pH ≈ const; Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH=pKa+log([A⁻]/[HA])

Concept: Increasing the number of pi electrons in a conjugated aromatic system increases aromaticity, stabilizing the molecule and making electrophilic aromatic substitution preferred.
Notes: ↑ π electrons in aromatic system → ↑ aromaticity → ↑ stability → EAS preferred over addition; Hückel 4n+2

Concept: As pressure in a gas increases at constant temperature, molecules are pushed closer together, increasing intermolecular forces and causing deviation from ideal behavior.
Notes: ↑ P (const T) → ↓ molecular distance → ↑ intermolecular forces → deviation from ideal gas; Z>1 or Z<1 depending on forces

Concept: Photoexcitation of a semiconductor raises electrons to the conduction band; recombination with holes releases energy as photons, the basis of LED and laser operation.
Notes: photon → e⁻ excited to conduction band; e⁻+hole recombine → photon emitted; E_photon ≈ bandgap; basis of LED

Concept: When a magnetic dipole is placed in an external magnetic field, it precesses around the field at the Larmor frequency, the basis of nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy.
Notes: magnetic dipole in B field → precession at Larmor freq (ω=γB); basis of NMR; ↑ B → ↑ precession freq

Concept: Deprotonating an alpha carbon adjacent to a carbonyl group forms a resonance-stabilized enolate anion that can act as a nucleophile in aldol condensation reactions.
Notes: base + α-H (adjacent to C=O) → enolate anion; resonance-stabilized; nucleophilic at α-carbon → aldol rxn

Concept: When dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens exceeds baseline prediction, the brain encodes a positive reward prediction error, reinforcing the behavior that preceded it.
Notes: DA > baseline in nucleus accumbens → ↑ reward prediction error (RPE) → behavior reinforced; VTA → mesolimbic

Concept: Blocking serotonin reuptake with SSRIs increases synaptic serotonin concentration, which over weeks desensitizes postsynaptic receptors and produces antidepressant effects.
Notes: SSRI → blocks 5-HT reuptake → ↑ synaptic 5-HT → weeks → receptor desensitization → antidepressant effect

Concept: When cortisol is chronically elevated due to stress, it damages hippocampal neurons by increasing glutamate excitotoxicity, impairing memory and spatial navigation.
Notes: chronic stress → ↑ cortisol → ↑ glutamate excitotoxicity in hippocampus → neuronal damage → ↓ memory + navigation

Concept: As sleep deprivation accumulates, adenosine builds up in the basal forebrain, progressively increasing sleep pressure until sleep occurs and adenosine is cleared.
Notes: sleep deprivation → ↑ adenosine (basal forebrain) → ↑ sleep pressure; sleep → adenosine cleared; caffeine blocks A1 receptor

Concept: Fear extinction occurs when the conditioned stimulus is repeatedly presented without the unconditioned stimulus, allowing the prefrontal cortex to inhibit amygdala fear responses.
Notes: CS w/o US repeated → PFC → inhibits amygdala → fear response ↓; extinction ≠ forgetting; IL cortex mediates

Concept: Norepinephrine released during acute stress activates locus coeruleus projections throughout the cortex, increasing arousal, attention, and alertness while narrowing attentional focus.
Notes: acute stress → locus coeruleus → NE release cortex-wide → ↑ arousal + ↑ attention; attentional focus narrows

Concept: When mirror neurons in the premotor cortex fire during observation of an action, they create a motor simulation in the observer, facilitating understanding of another's intentions.
Notes: action observed → mirror neurons (premotor) fire → motor simulation → ↑ intent understanding; empathy basis

Concept: Inactivating the ventromedial prefrontal cortex in animal studies impairs fear extinction but not acquisition, demonstrating its selective role in suppressing learned fear.
Notes: vmPFC inactivation → ↓ fear extinction; fear acquisition unchanged; ∴ vmPFC = selective role in suppressing learned fear

Concept: Elevated glutamate in schizophrenia's NMDA hypofunction model explains negative symptoms because reduced NMDA on inhibitory interneurons disinhibits cortical excitatory neurons.
Notes: ↓ NMDA on GABAergic interneurons → disinhibition of cortical excitatory neurons → ↑ glutamate dysregulation → negative symptoms (schizophrenia)

Concept: As task difficulty increases beyond cognitive capacity, working memory load exceeds the central executive's capacity, causing performance to drop sharply.
Notes: ↑ task difficulty → working memory load > central executive capacity → performance ↓ sharply; cognitive overload

Concept: Chronic alcohol use downregulates GABA-A receptors and upregulates NMDA receptors; abrupt withdrawal causes excess excitation, leading to seizures and delirium tremens.
Notes: chronic alcohol → ↓ GABA-A + ↑ NMDA receptors; abrupt withdrawal → excess excitation → seizures + DTs

Concept: When theta oscillations synchronize between the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex, the temporal coupling facilitates transfer of spatial memory during navigation.
Notes: hippocampus-PFC theta synchrony → ↑ temporal coupling → ↑ spatial memory transfer; phase coherence ↔ learning

Concept: Transcranial magnetic stimulation over the right temporoparietal junction disrupts the sense of body ownership, temporarily creating out-of-body-like experiences.
Notes: TMS over right TPJ → ↓ body ownership sense → out-of-body experience; TPJ ↔ multisensory self-location

Concept: Infants raised without adequate sensory and social stimulation show blunted stress responses and structural changes in the prefrontal cortex, impairing later emotional regulation.
Notes: early sensory/social deprivation → ↓ stress response + PFC structural ↓ → ↓ emotional regulation later life

Concept: Activation of mu-opioid receptors in the periaqueductal gray inhibits GABAergic interneurons, disinhibiting descending serotonergic and noradrenergic pain modulation pathways.
Notes: mu-opioid → PAG → inhibits GABA interneurons → disinhibition → ↑ descending 5-HT + NE → ↓ pain

Concept: As practice increases on a motor task, activity shifts from the prefrontal cortex and striatum toward the cerebellum and motor cortex, reflecting automatization.
Notes: ↑ practice → activity: PFC + striatum → cerebellum + motor cortex; automatization; ↓ cognitive load required

Concept: When the lateral hypothalamus is lesioned, animals stop eating despite food availability, demonstrating that this region acts as a hunger-promoting center.
Notes: lateral hypothalamus lesion → ↓ eating w/ food available; ∴ LH = hunger center; ≠ VMH lesion (↑ eating)

Concept: Increased dopamine signaling in the mesolimbic pathway causes positive symptoms of schizophrenia, whereas reduced dopamine in the mesocortical pathway causes negative symptoms.
Notes: ↑ DA mesolimbic → positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions); ↓ DA mesocortical → negative symptoms (flat affect); schizophrenia

Concept: Repeated exposure to a stimulus without consequence causes habituation, reducing response amplitude, while dishabituation occurs when a novel stimulus resets the response.
Notes: repeated stimulus → habituation → ↓ response amplitude; novel stimulus → dishabituation → response resets

Concept: Damage to the right parietal cortex causes hemispatial neglect where patients ignore the left side of space, demonstrating contralateral spatial attention mapping.
Notes: right parietal damage → left hemispatial neglect; ∵ right hemisphere maps both sides; left hemisphere only right side

Concept: When a conditioned cue is presented in an extinction context, fear can spontaneously recover in the original context, showing that extinction does not erase memory.
Notes: extinction in new context → fear suppressed; return to original context → spontaneous recovery; ∴ extinction ≠ erasure

Concept: Long-term depression in the cerebellum occurs when parallel fiber and climbing fiber inputs are activated simultaneously, weakening the parallel fiber to Purkinje cell synapse.
Notes: parallel fiber + climbing fiber simultaneous → LTD at parallel fiber-Purkinje cell synapse → ↓ synaptic strength; motor learning

Concept: High levels of BDNF promote dendritic branching and synaptogenesis, whereas low BDNF in depression correlates with hippocampal atrophy and impaired neurogenesis.
Notes: ↑ BDNF → ↑ dendritic branching + synaptogenesis; ↓ BDNF (depression) → hippocampal atrophy + ↓ neurogenesis

Concept: Activation of orexin/hypocretin neurons in the lateral hypothalamus promotes wakefulness; loss of these neurons in narcolepsy causes sudden sleep attacks and cataplexy.
Notes: orexin neurons (LH) → ↑ wakefulness; orexin neuron loss → narcolepsy → sleep attacks + cataplexy; ↓ CSF orexin

Concept: When the anterior cingulate cortex detects conflict between response options, it signals the prefrontal cortex to increase cognitive control, slowing response and reducing errors.
Notes: conflict detected → ACC → signals PFC → ↑ cognitive control → ↓ response speed + ↓ error rate; Stroop

Concept: When the central bank reduces the policy interest rate, commercial banks lower their lending rates, increasing borrowing, stimulating investment and consumption.
Notes: ↓ policy rate → ↓ lending rates → ↑ borrowing → ↑ investment + ↑ consumption → ↑ GDP; monetary transmission

Concept: A government budget deficit requires financing through bond issuance; if the central bank monetizes this, money supply increases, potentially causing inflation.
Notes: govt deficit → bond issuance; if CB monetizes → ↑ money supply → ↑ inflation risk; vs. non-monetized: ↑ interest rates

Concept: When nominal wages fail to adjust downward during a recession, unemployment rises above the natural rate because labor markets cannot clear at reduced prices.
Notes: ↓ nominal wages rigid → labor market doesn't clear → unemployment > natural rate; sticky wages (Keynesian)

Concept: Rising asset prices increase household wealth, which raises consumption through the wealth effect, providing a positive feedback channel into aggregate demand.
Notes: ↑ asset prices → ↑ household wealth → ↑ consumption (wealth effect) → ↑ aggregate demand → ↑ GDP

Concept: When domestic inflation exceeds that of trading partners, the real exchange rate appreciates, making exports more expensive and reducing trade competitiveness.
Notes: ↑ domestic inflation > trading partners → ↑ real exchange rate → ↑ export prices → ↓ export competitiveness

Concept: Increasing the reserve requirement for banks reduces the money multiplier, contracting the money supply and raising interest rates in the short run.
Notes: ↑ reserve requirement → ↓ money multiplier (1/r) → ↓ money supply → ↑ interest rates (SR)

Concept: When oil prices rise, production costs increase across industries, shifting aggregate supply left and causing stagflation with both higher prices and lower output.
Notes: ↑ oil prices → ↑ production costs → AS shifts left → ↑ P + ↓ Y + ↑ unemployment; stagflation

Concept: As income inequality increases, those at the bottom of the distribution have higher marginal propensity to consume; redistribution from rich to poor increases aggregate demand.
Notes: ↑ inequality → poor have higher MPC; redistribution (rich → poor) → ↑ aggregate demand; ∵ MPC_poor > MPC_rich

Concept: Devaluing a currency makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive; if the Marshall-Lerner condition holds, the trade balance improves over time after an initial J-curve deterioration.
Notes: ↓ currency → ↓ export prices + ↑ import prices; if Marshall-Lerner: ↑ trade balance LR; J-curve: ↓ SR then ↑ LR

Concept: When firms are price takers in a competitive market, they produce until marginal cost equals the market price; producing more would reduce profit since MC exceeds price.
Notes: competitive firm: produce until MC = P; MC > P → ↓ profit by producing more; MC < P → ↑ profit by producing more

Concept: Rising interest rates increase the opportunity cost of holding non-yielding assets like gold and growth stocks, causing their prices to fall as investors shift to bonds.
Notes: ↑ interest rates → ↑ opportunity cost of non-yielding assets → ↓ gold + ↓ growth stock prices; ↑ bond attractiveness

Concept: As more firms enter a competitive market in response to economic profit, supply increases, prices fall, and profit is competed away to zero in the long run.
Notes: economic profit → ↑ firm entry → ↑ supply → ↓ price → profit → 0; LR competitive equilibrium

Concept: When consumers have lower time preference, they save more, lowering the real interest rate and increasing the capital stock available for investment.
Notes: ↓ time preference → ↑ savings → ↓ real interest rate → ↑ investment → ↑ capital stock; Ramsey model

Concept: Trade liberalization between countries with different relative prices causes specialization in comparative advantage goods, raising total output but creating distributional winners and losers.
Notes: trade liberalization → specialization by comparative advantage → ↑ total output; distributional effects: winners ≠ losers

Concept: Increasing corporate tax rates reduces after-tax returns on investment, lowering investment, reducing capital accumulation, and potentially reducing wages in the long run.
Notes: ↑ corporate tax → ↓ after-tax returns → ↓ investment → ↓ capital accumulation → ↓ wages LR

Concept: When information asymmetry causes adverse selection in insurance markets, high-risk individuals are more likely to purchase insurance, raising average costs and premiums.
Notes: info asymmetry → adverse selection: high-risk more likely to insure → ↑ avg cost → ↑ premiums → market unraveling

Concept: As the economy moves into expansion, automatic stabilizers like progressive taxes increase and transfer payments decrease, automatically reducing the fiscal stimulus.
Notes: economic expansion → ↑ tax revenue (progressive) + ↓ transfers → ↓ fiscal stimulus automatically; built-in stabilizers

Concept: When a monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve, marginal revenue is less than price, causing underproduction relative to the competitive equilibrium and creating deadweight loss.
Notes: monopolist: MR < P → produce where MR=MC → ↓ output vs competitive → deadweight loss; P > MC

Concept: Excessive bank leverage amplifies returns in good times but amplifies losses in bad times, creating systemic risk when correlated asset declines threaten solvency.
Notes: ↑ bank leverage → ↑ returns (good times) + ↑ losses (bad times); correlated ↓ assets → systemic insolvency risk

Concept: Increasing minimum wages above market equilibrium reduces employment in competitive labor markets but may increase employment in monopsonistic markets by counteracting wage suppression.
Notes: ↑ minimum wage above equilibrium: competitive market → ↓ employment; monopsony → ↑ employment; ∴ effect ∝ market structure

Concept: When capital is freely mobile across borders, a small open economy cannot use monetary policy to independently set interest rates different from the world interest rate.
Notes: free capital mobility + small open economy → domestic interest rate = world rate; ∴ monetary policy ineffective; Mundell-Fleming

Concept: Rising house prices increase household collateral values, enabling more borrowing, which funds consumption, further raising prices in a self-reinforcing cycle until a shock breaks it.
Notes: ↑ house prices → ↑ collateral → ↑ borrowing → ↑ consumption → ↑ prices; positive feedback; vulnerable to reversal

Concept: When the exchange rate is fixed and capital flows freely, an expansionary fiscal policy is fully effective because capital inflows prevent interest rate rises from crowding out investment.
Notes: fixed FX + free capital: fiscal expansion → ↑ G → ↑ interest rate → capital inflow → currency pressure → CB buys foreign → ↑ M → full effectiveness

Concept: As productivity growth slows in an economy, potential GDP growth falls, wages stagnate, and without policy intervention, living standards improve more slowly.
Notes: ↓ productivity growth → ↓ potential GDP growth → stagnant wages → ↓ living standard improvement; secular stagnation

Concept: When the debt-to-GDP ratio rises sufficiently, markets demand higher risk premiums on government bonds, raising borrowing costs and potentially triggering a debt spiral.
Notes: ↑ debt/GDP → ↑ risk premium → ↑ borrowing costs → ↑ debt even faster → debt spiral risk; fiscal sustainability threshold

Concept: Increasing financial market integration between countries transmits shocks more quickly; a crisis originating in one country can rapidly spread through correlated asset prices.
Notes: ↑ financial integration → ↑ shock transmission speed; crisis in one country → correlated assets → rapid contagion

Concept: When consumers anchor expectations of future prices to past prices, they adapt more slowly to new inflation regimes, making disinflationary policy more costly in lost output.
Notes: expectations anchored to past → slow adjustment to new inflation regime → ↑ sacrifice ratio; adaptive ≠ rational expectations

Concept: A positive technology shock increases total factor productivity, shifting the production possibilities frontier outward and raising real wages and the return to capital.
Notes: ↑ TFP (tech shock) → PPF shifts out → ↑ real wages + ↑ return to capital + ↑ potential output

Concept: When governments subsidize higher education, human capital investment increases, raising future labor productivity, wages, and tax revenues that may exceed the initial subsidy cost.
Notes: govt education subsidy → ↑ human capital investment → ↑ labor productivity → ↑ wages → ↑ tax revenue; potential positive ROI

Concept: In a prisoner's dilemma played repeatedly with the same partners, cooperation can emerge through conditional strategies like tit-for-tat, unlike the one-shot game.
Notes: repeated prisoner's dilemma → tit-for-tat sustains cooperation; ≠ one-shot (dominant strategy = defect); shadow of future matters

Concept: When carbon is priced through a tax or cap-and-trade system, firms internalize the external cost of emissions, reducing pollution to the socially optimal level.
Notes: carbon price → internalize external cost → ↓ emissions to socially optimal level; Pigou tax or cap-and-trade

Concept: Rising uncertainty causes firms to defer irreversible investments, reducing aggregate demand even when interest rates are low, amplifying recessions through the option value of waiting.
Notes: ↑ uncertainty → ↓ irreversible investment (wait option valuable) → ↓ aggregate demand; amplifies recession even w/ ↓ rates

Concept: Increasing trade with low-wage countries raises consumer surplus through lower prices but reduces wages for import-competing workers, creating trade-offs that redistribution could address.
Notes: ↑ trade w/ low-wage countries → ↑ consumer surplus (↓ prices) + ↓ wages (import-competing workers); redistribution needed

Concept: When expectations of future income rise, consumption rises today through permanent income smoothing, reducing the effectiveness of temporary fiscal transfers in boosting spending.
Notes: ↑ expected future income → ↑ current consumption via permanent income hypothesis; temporary transfers → ↓ multiplier effect

Concept: When the learning rate is set too high in gradient descent, parameter updates overshoot the minimum, causing the loss to oscillate or diverge rather than converge.
Notes: ↑ learning rate → overshoot minimum → loss oscillates or diverges; ≠ ↓ lr (slow convergence); optimal lr ↔ convergence speed

Concept: Increasing the depth of a neural network allows it to learn more abstract hierarchical features but introduces vanishing gradients that impede learning in early layers.
Notes: ↑ depth → ↑ abstract feature learning; vanishing gradients → ↓ learning in early layers; fix: ResNet/batch norm/ReLU

Concept: When the training dataset is too small relative to model complexity, the model memorizes noise in training data, performing well on training but poorly on unseen data.
Notes: small data + complex model → memorize noise → ↑ train accuracy; ↓ test accuracy; overfitting; fix: regularize/↑ data

Concept: Removing batch normalization from a deep network causes internal covariate shift, where the distribution of each layer's input changes during training, slowing convergence.
Notes: w/o batch norm → internal covariate shift → layer input distribution ↑ variability → ↓ convergence speed

Concept: When two threads access shared memory concurrently without synchronization, a race condition can cause unpredictable outcomes depending on the order of execution.
Notes: concurrent threads + shared memory w/o sync → race condition → unpredictable output; fix: mutex/semaphore/atomic ops

Concept: Increasing the vocabulary size in a language model increases its representational capacity for rare words but also increases the number of parameters in the embedding layer.
Notes: ↑ vocab size → ↑ rare word representation + ↑ embedding parameters; memory ↑; ∴ trade-off w/ compute + coverage

Concept: When a hash function maps many keys to the same bucket, the collision chain grows, causing lookup time to degrade from O(1) average to O(n) worst case.
Notes: poor hash fn → many collisions → ↑ chain length → O(n) worst-case lookup; ≠ O(1) avg; fix: better hash fn + resize

Concept: Increasing the number of attention heads in a transformer allows it to attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously, improving contextual understanding.
Notes: ↑ attention heads → ↑ subspace diversity → ↑ contextual understanding; each head attends to different relations

Concept: When a distributed system experiences a network partition, it must choose between maintaining consistency (refusing requests) or availability (serving potentially stale data).
Notes: network partition → choose: consistency (refuse requests) or availability (serve stale data); CAP theorem; ≠ both

Concept: Increasing the model context window for a transformer increases the quadratic attention computation cost, making inference slower and more memory-intensive.
Notes: ↑ context window → ↑ attention cost O(n²) → ↑ inference latency + ↑ memory; ∴ efficient attention needed for long seqs

Concept: When database indices are not updated after bulk inserts, query plans that rely on index statistics become suboptimal, causing full table scans and slow queries.
Notes: bulk insert w/o index update → stale statistics → suboptimal query plan → full table scan → ↑ query time; run ANALYZE

Concept: Increasing the number of nodes in a distributed cluster increases fault tolerance because data can be replicated across more nodes, reducing single-point-of-failure risk.
Notes: ↑ cluster nodes → ↑ replication options → ↑ fault tolerance → ↓ single-point-of-failure risk; ∝ replication factor

Concept: When a convolutional neural network uses a larger kernel size, it can capture broader spatial patterns but requires more parameters and reduces spatial resolution faster.
Notes: ↑ CNN kernel size → ↑ spatial pattern scope + ↑ parameters + ↓ spatial resolution; trade-off: receptive field vs. compute

Concept: Applying dropout during training randomly zeroes activations, forcing the network to learn redundant representations, which reduces overfitting and improves generalization.
Notes: dropout: random activation → 0 during training → redundant representations → ↓ overfitting → ↑ generalization

Concept: When a message queue fills up because consumers are slower than producers, back pressure mechanisms must signal producers to slow down or messages will be dropped.
Notes: producer > consumer → queue fills → back pressure signals producer to ↓ rate; w/o back pressure → message drop

Concept: Increasing the temperature parameter in a language model's sampling makes the probability distribution flatter, generating more diverse but less coherent text.
Notes: ↑ temperature → flatter prob distribution → ↑ diversity; ↓ coherence; T→0 = greedy; T→∞ = uniform random

Concept: When a machine learning model is deployed on production data that differs from training data distribution, model performance degrades due to covariate shift.
Notes: production data ≠ training distribution → covariate shift → ↓ model performance; fix: domain adaptation + monitoring

Concept: Adding more training data to a simple model reduces variance but cannot reduce bias if the model is fundamentally too simple to capture the true relationship.
Notes: ↑ training data → ↓ variance; bias unchanged if model too simple; ∴ data alone cannot fix high-bias model

Concept: When a database query uses a nested loop join on large unindexed tables, time complexity is O(n×m), but a hash join reduces this to O(n+m) with appropriate memory.
Notes: nested loop join (unindexed): O(n×m); hash join: O(n+m); ↑ table size → hash join ↑ advantage; memory trade-off

Concept: Increasing regularization strength in a model shrinks coefficient magnitudes toward zero, reducing model complexity and variance at the cost of increased bias.
Notes: ↑ regularization → ↓ coefficient magnitudes → ↓ model complexity + ↓ variance + ↑ bias; bias-variance trade-off

Concept: When a load balancer uses round-robin scheduling, it distributes requests evenly across servers, but long-running requests can cause uneven actual load if not considered.
Notes: round-robin LB → even request distribution; long-running requests → uneven actual load; ∴ least-connections better for varied latency

Concept: Decreasing the batch size in stochastic gradient descent introduces more noise in gradient estimates, potentially escaping local minima but reducing computational efficiency.
Notes: ↓ batch size → ↑ gradient noise → potential local minima escape; ↓ compute efficiency; SGD ≠ full-batch GD

Concept: When an NLP model is trained only on English text and tested on other languages, performance drops dramatically because language-specific patterns were not learned.
Notes: train English only → test other languages → ↓ performance; language-specific patterns ≠ cross-lingual; fix: multilingual training

Concept: Increasing cache size reduces cache misses and memory access latency, improving program performance up to the point where the working set fits entirely in cache.
Notes: ↑ cache size → ↓ cache misses → ↓ memory latency → ↑ performance; diminishing returns once working set fits

Concept: When two microservices communicate synchronously, a failure in one propagates to the other; making communication asynchronous via message queues isolates failures.
Notes: sync microservice comm: failure A → failure B; async (message queue) → isolate failures; ↑ resilience; ↓ coupling

Concept: Applying gradient clipping prevents exploding gradients by capping the gradient norm, allowing stable training of recurrent networks on long sequences.
Notes: gradient clipping → cap gradient norm → ↓ exploding gradients → stable RNN training on long sequences

Concept: When the learning rate is annealed over training, the model makes large updates early to explore the loss landscape and smaller updates late to fine-tune near the minimum.
Notes: lr annealing: large updates early (exploration) → small updates late (fine-tuning near minimum); ↑ convergence quality

Concept: Increasing the depth of a decision tree reduces bias but increases variance; pruning reverses this by removing nodes that add complexity without improving generalization.
Notes: ↑ tree depth → ↓ bias + ↑ variance; pruning → ↑ bias + ↓ variance; ∴ bias-variance trade-off via depth control

Concept: When a model uses weight sharing across positions as in a convolutional network, it generalizes spatial patterns to different locations, dramatically reducing parameter count.
Notes: weight sharing (CNN) → spatial pattern generalizes across positions → ↓ parameters × 1/num_positions; ↑ parameter efficiency

Concept: Distributing model layers across multiple GPUs via pipeline parallelism allows training larger models but introduces pipeline bubbles where GPUs idle waiting for earlier stages.
Notes: pipeline parallelism → ↑ model size possible; pipeline bubbles → GPU idle time; ↓ efficiency vs. data parallelism

Concept: Adding residual connections to a deep network allows gradients to flow directly to earlier layers, addressing the vanishing gradient problem and enabling training of very deep networks.
Notes: residual connections: gradient highway → early layers; ↓ vanishing gradient; enables very deep networks (ResNet 152+)

Concept: When tokens in a sequence are encoded with absolute positional encodings, the model can distinguish position but cannot generalize to longer sequences than seen in training.
Notes: absolute positional encoding → distinguishes position in train range; ↓ generalization to longer seqs; ≠ relative/RoPE

Concept: Increasing model width (neurons per layer) allows the model to represent more patterns simultaneously but increases memory usage and computational cost quadratically.
Notes: ↑ width → ↑ representational capacity + ↑ memory + ↑ compute ∝ width²; ≠ depth scaling (exponential capacity)

Concept: When a graph neural network aggregates neighbor information, adding more layers allows messages to propagate further but causes over-smoothing if too many layers are used.
Notes: ↑ GNN layers → ↑ message propagation distance; too many layers → over-smoothing → node representations ≈ same

Concept: Applying label smoothing during training replaces the hard one-hot targets with soft distributions, preventing the model from becoming overconfident and improving calibration.
Notes: label smoothing: hard targets → soft (ε spread); ↓ overconfidence → ↑ calibration + ↑ generalization; ε=0.1 typical

Concept: When a ranking model optimizes pointwise loss it treats each item independently, but pairwise or listwise losses leverage the relative ordering information, improving ranking quality.
Notes: pointwise loss: items independent; pairwise: relative ordering → ↑ ranking quality; listwise: full list context → best but costlier

Concept: Increasing the number of negative samples in contrastive learning improves the quality of learned representations by providing more discrimination targets per positive pair.
Notes: ↑ negative samples (contrastive) → ↑ discrimination targets → ↑ representation quality; SimCLR/InfoNCE benefit

Concept: When a foundation model is fine-tuned with too high a learning rate, it catastrophically forgets pre-trained knowledge, making the fine-tuned model worse than the original.
Notes: ↑ lr in fine-tuning → catastrophic forgetting of pretrain knowledge → ↓ performance; fix: ↓ lr + LoRA + frozen layers

Concept: Sparse attention patterns reduce the quadratic cost of full self-attention to approximately linear, enabling transformers to process much longer sequences efficiently.
Notes: sparse attention → O(n²) → O(n log n) or O(n); ↑ sequence length feasible; Longformer/BigBird patterns

Concept: When the number of parameters in a model exceeds the amount of training data, the model can fit any label assignment, but it may still generalize well with proper regularization.
Notes: parameters > training data → can memorize any labels; may still generalize w/ regularization (double descent phenomenon)

Concept: When blood pressure rises, baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch increase their firing rate, signaling the brainstem to reduce sympathetic and increase parasympathetic output.
Notes: ↑ BP → ↑ baroreceptor firing → brainstem → ↓ sympathetic + ↑ parasympathetic → ↓ HR + ↓ vasomotor tone → ↓ BP

Concept: Decreased oxygen delivery to the kidney activates the juxtaglomerular apparatus to release renin, triggering the RAAS cascade and ultimately raising blood pressure.
Notes: ↓ renal O₂ → ↑ renin (JGA) → angiotensinogen → AngI → ACE → AngII → vasoconstriction + ↑ aldosterone → ↑ BP

Concept: In type 1 diabetes, destruction of beta cells eliminates insulin production, causing hyperglycemia, osmotic diuresis, polydipsia, and eventually ketoacidosis without insulin therapy.
