Notes: β-cell destruction → ↓ insulin → ↑ glucose → osmotic diuresis → polyuria + polydipsia; w/o insulin → DKA

Concept: When a patient has an acute MI, reduced cardiac output activates the sympathetic nervous system and RAAS, causing compensatory tachycardia, vasoconstriction, and sodium retention.
Notes: acute MI → ↓ CO → ↑ sympathetic + ↑ RAAS → compensatory tachycardia + vasoconstriction + ↑ Na⁺ retention

Concept: Elevated serum troponin levels after chest pain indicate cardiomyocyte death because troponin leaks into the bloodstream only when the cell membrane is disrupted.
Notes: ↑ serum troponin → cardiomyocyte membrane disrupted → troponin leaked; ∴ specific marker of myocardial necrosis

Concept: As glomerular filtration rate decreases in chronic kidney disease, phosphate retention rises, parathyroid hormone increases, calcium falls, and renal osteodystrophy develops.
Notes: ↓ GFR → ↑ phosphate → ↓ calcium → ↑ PTH → renal osteodystrophy; also ↓ calcitriol → ↓ Ca²⁺ absorption

Concept: When cortisol is chronically elevated as in Cushing's syndrome, it causes central obesity, muscle wasting, hyperglycemia, and immunosuppression through multiple receptor-mediated effects.
Notes: chronic ↑ cortisol (Cushing's) → central obesity + muscle wasting + ↑ glucose + immunosuppression; multiple tissue effects

Concept: Blocking ACE with an ACE inhibitor prevents conversion of angiotensin I to angiotensin II, reducing vasoconstriction and aldosterone release, thereby lowering blood pressure.
Notes: ACE inhibitor → ↓ AngI→AngII conversion → ↓ vasoconstriction + ↓ aldosterone → ↓ Na⁺ retention → ↓ BP

Concept: When aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase in platelets, thromboxane A2 production stops for the life of the platelet (7–10 days), reducing platelet aggregation.
Notes: aspirin → irreversible COX inhibition → ↓ TXA2 → ↓ platelet aggregation; effect lasts 7-10 days (platelet lifespan)

Concept: Rising serum bilirubin causes jaundice when it exceeds 2–3 mg/dL; unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia suggests hemolysis or liver uptake defect, while conjugated suggests biliary obstruction.
Notes: ↑ bilirubin >2-3 mg/dL → jaundice; unconjugated ↑ → hemolysis or ↓ uptake; conjugated ↑ → biliary obstruction

Concept: When left ventricular pressure exceeds aortic pressure, the aortic valve opens and blood is ejected; when it falls below, the valve closes, marking end of systole.
Notes: LV P > aortic P → aortic valve opens → ejection; LV P < aortic P → aortic valve closes → end systole; Wiggers diagram

Concept: Thiazide diuretics block the sodium-chloride cotransporter in the distal tubule, reducing sodium and water reabsorption, lowering blood volume and blood pressure.
Notes: thiazide → blocks NCC (distal tubule) → ↓ Na⁺/water reabsorption → ↓ blood volume → ↓ BP; side effect: ↓ K⁺

Concept: As pulmonary arterial pressure rises in pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular afterload increases, causing right ventricular hypertrophy and eventually right heart failure.
Notes: ↑ pulmonary arterial pressure → ↑ RV afterload → RV hypertrophy → RV failure (cor pulmonale)

Concept: When FEV1/FVC ratio is reduced below 0.7 on spirometry, it confirms obstructive physiology characteristic of asthma or COPD, distinguishing it from restrictive disease.
Notes: FEV1/FVC <0.7 → obstructive (asthma/COPD); ≠ restrictive (FEV1/FVC ≥0.7 but ↓ FVC); spirometry distinction

Concept: Statins reduce LDL cholesterol by inhibiting HMG-CoA reductase in the liver, reducing cholesterol synthesis, upregulating LDL receptors, and increasing LDL uptake from blood.
Notes: statin → ↓ HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ cholesterol synthesis → ↑ LDL receptors → ↑ LDL uptake → ↓ serum LDL

Concept: In anaphylaxis, massive IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation releases histamine and other mediators causing vasodilation, bronchoconstriction, and vascular leak, requiring epinephrine.
Notes: anaphylaxis: IgE → mast cell degranulation → ↑ histamine → vasodilation + bronchoconstriction + vascular leak; Rx: epinephrine

Concept: When oxygen saturation falls below 90%, the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve enters the steep portion, and small further decreases in pO2 cause large drops in saturation.
Notes: SpO₂ <90% → steep portion oxyhemoglobin curve; small ↓ pO₂ → large ↓ saturation; clinical danger threshold

Concept: Increased intracranial pressure compresses cerebral perfusion, and when ICP approaches mean arterial pressure, cerebral blood flow falls critically, causing ischemia.
Notes: ↑ ICP → CPP = MAP - ICP ↓; if ICP → MAP → CBF → 0 → cerebral ischemia; herniation risk

Concept: When potassium rises above 6 mEq/L, cardiac membrane potential is altered, causing peaked T waves, then QRS widening, and ultimately ventricular fibrillation if untreated.
Notes: K⁺ >6 mEq/L → ↑ resting membrane potential → peaked T → QRS widening → VF; Rx: Ca²⁺ (stabilize membrane) + insulin

Concept: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase, preventing recycling of vitamin K, which is required for gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X.
Notes: warfarin → ↓ VKORC1 → ↓ vitamin K recycling → ↓ γ-carboxylation → ↓ factors II/VII/IX/X → ↑ INR → anticoagulation

Concept: In septic shock, vasodilation reduces systemic vascular resistance dramatically; despite high cardiac output, tissues are not adequately perfused due to maldistribution.
Notes: septic shock: ↑ vasodilation → ↓ SVR; ↑ CO initially; maldistribution → ↓ tissue perfusion despite ↑ CO; warm shock

Concept: Rising serum creatinine reflects falling GFR because creatinine is freely filtered and not reabsorbed; doubling creatinine roughly halves GFR.
Notes: ↑ serum creatinine ≈ ↓ GFR; creatinine freely filtered + not reabsorbed; creatinine ×2 → GFR ÷2; CKD staging

Concept: When hypoxemia is refractory to supplemental oxygen, intrapulmonary shunting is the likely cause because shunted blood never contacts alveolar oxygen.
Notes: refractory hypoxemia w/ O₂ → shunt; shunted blood bypasses alveoli → O₂ cannot correct; V/Q=0; diagnose w/ 100% O₂ test

Concept: Proton pump inhibitors irreversibly inhibit the H+/K+ ATPase in parietal cells, reducing acid secretion regardless of stimulation type; effective for peptic ulcer disease.
Notes: PPI → irreversible H⁺/K⁺ ATPase inhibition (parietal cells) → ↓ acid regardless of stimulus; peptic ulcer Rx

Concept: When thyroid-stimulating hormone is suppressed and free T4 is elevated, hyperthyroidism is confirmed; if TSH is elevated with low T4, primary hypothyroidism is present.
Notes: ↓ TSH + ↑ fT4 → hyperthyroidism; ↑ TSH + ↓ fT4 → primary hypothyroidism; TSH = most sensitive initial test

Concept: When the printing press spread across Europe after 1450, literacy rates rose, vernacular languages gained prestige, and the Catholic Church lost its monopoly on biblical interpretation.
Notes: printing press post-1450 → ↑ literacy + ↑ vernacular prestige → ↓ Church monopoly on interpretation → Reformation

Concept: As colonial powers drew arbitrary borders in Africa at the Berlin Conference, they split ethnic groups across states and combined hostile groups, creating long-term conflict drivers.
Notes: Berlin Conference 1884 → arbitrary African borders → split ethnic groups + combined hostile groups → ↑ post-colonial conflict

Concept: The failure of the League of Nations to stop Japanese aggression in Manchuria in 1931 demonstrated that collective security without enforcement mechanisms is ineffective.
Notes: League of Nations + Manchuria 1931: collective security w/o enforcement → Japan unpunished → ↓ League credibility → failure

Concept: When Mikhail Gorbachev reduced Soviet subsidies to Eastern European satellite states, their economies weakened and popular discontent rose, accelerating the 1989 revolutions.
Notes: Gorbachev ↓ Soviet subsidies → Eastern Europe econ ↓ → popular discontent ↑ → 1989 revolutions; Sinatra Doctrine

Concept: Rising inequality during the Gilded Age in the United States concentrated political power alongside economic power, leading to regulatory capture and weakening democratic accountability.
Notes: ↑ inequality (Gilded Age US) → concentrated economic power → ↑ political power → regulatory capture → ↓ democratic accountability

Concept: When authoritarian governments allow limited pluralism to manage elites but not mass participation, they create hybrid regimes that are more durable than pure autocracies under certain conditions.
Notes: limited pluralism + ↓ mass participation → hybrid regime; more durable than pure autocracy under certain conditions; Linz

Concept: As the Cold War shifted to economic competition after détente, the United States promoted neoliberal institutions like the IMF and World Bank to embed market economies globally.
Notes: Cold War → détente → economic competition → US promotes neoliberal institutions (IMF/WB) → market economies embedded globally

Concept: When democracies fail to deliver economic growth, voters shift support toward populist parties that promise to bypass institutions, threatening liberal democratic norms.
Notes: democracy fails to ↑ econ growth → voter discontent → ↑ populist support → bypassing institutions → ↓ liberal norms

Concept: The invention of gunpowder weapons shifted military advantage from cavalry-based feudal armies toward infantry and siege warfare, undermining the social basis of feudalism.
Notes: gunpowder weapons → ↑ infantry/siege advantage → ↓ cavalry advantage → feudal military basis undermined → ↓ feudalism

Concept: When nation-states failed to satisfy the cultural and political aspirations of minorities within their borders, secessionist movements grew, often leading to violent conflict.
Notes: nation-state fails minority aspirations → ↑ secessionist movements → ↑ violent conflict probability; Kurdish/Balkan examples

Concept: Increasing global financial integration after Bretton Woods II allowed capital to flow to high-return countries but also amplified contagion when crises occurred.
Notes: ↑ global financial integration post-Bretton Woods II → ↑ capital mobility to high-return regions + ↑ contagion during crises

Concept: The Kantian peace hypothesis argues that as the number of democratic dyads increases globally, the probability of interstate war decreases, supported by empirical evidence.
Notes: ↑ democratic dyads globally → ↓ interstate war probability; democratic peace hypothesis; Kant + empirical support

Concept: When property rights are poorly defined, individuals over-exploit common resources because private benefits of extraction exceed their private share of depletion costs.
Notes: poorly defined property rights → private benefit > private share of depletion cost → over-exploitation; tragedy of commons; Ostrom fix

Concept: The spread of social media lowered the cost of collective action, allowing protesters to coordinate rapidly, but also enabled misinformation to spread and polarize electorates.
Notes: ↓ social media cost → ↑ collective action speed; ↑ misinformation spread → ↑ polarization; dual effect on democracy

Concept: As nuclear weapons proliferate to more states, deterrence theory predicts stability through mutually assured destruction, but also raises the probability of accidental war.
Notes: ↑ nuclear proliferation → ↑ deterrence stability (MAD) but ↑ accidental war risk; ∴ net effect ambiguous

Concept: When governments pursue austerity during recessions, declining aggregate demand reduces tax revenues, potentially increasing the deficit despite spending cuts, the paradox of thrift applied to government.
Notes: austerity during recession → ↓ aggregate demand → ↓ tax revenues → deficit may ↑ despite ↓ spending; paradox of thrift

Concept: Nietzsche's will to power argues that human beings are driven not primarily by pleasure-seeking but by the drive to overcome, express, and expand, rejecting utilitarian accounts.
Notes: Nietzsche will to power: humans driven by ↑ power/overcome/expand ≠ pleasure-seeking; rejects utilitarian psychology

Concept: When Rawls derives principles of justice from the original position, the veil of ignorance ensures that rational agents choose principles that protect the worst-off.
Notes: Rawls: original position + veil of ignorance → rational agents → protect worst-off (difference principle); ∵ risk aversion under uncertainty

Concept: Hegel's dialectic: a thesis generates its antithesis through internal contradiction; the synthesis resolves both and becomes a new thesis, driving historical progress.
Notes: Hegel dialectic: thesis → contradiction → antithesis → synthesis → new thesis; history = ↑ Geist through contradiction

Concept: Foucault's concept of biopower explains how modern states regulate populations not through direct coercion but through norms of health, sexuality, and productivity.
Notes: Foucault biopower: state regulates populations via health/sexuality/productivity norms ≠ direct coercion; disciplinary society

Concept: As artificial intelligence systems demonstrate superhuman performance in specific domains, questions of moral status and rights become philosophically pressing, challenging anthropocentric frameworks.
Notes: AI superhuman in domains → ↑ moral status questions → challenges anthropocentric ethics; ≠ current law; Turing/philosophy boundary

Concept: Hannah Arendt's banality of evil argues that the Holocaust was perpetrated not by monsters but by ordinary people following orders, highlighting the danger of thoughtlessness in bureaucratic systems.
Notes: Arendt banality of evil: Holocaust perpetrators = ordinary people following orders ≠ monsters; danger = thoughtlessness in bureaucracy

Concept: When international institutions are designed with veto players, decision-making becomes slower but outputs are more durable because more stakeholders have approved them.
Notes: ↑ veto players in institution → ↓ decision speed + ↑ output durability; ∵ more stakeholders approve → ↑ compliance

Concept: Derrida's deconstruction reveals that binary oppositions in language always privilege one term over the other, and that this hierarchy can be reversed and destabilized.
Notes: Derrida deconstruction: binary oppositions → privilege one term; hierarchy can be reversed → destabilized; différance

Concept: When institutions constrain executive power effectively, leaders cannot easily reverse policies, increasing policy credibility and attracting long-term investment.
Notes: effective institutional constraints → ↓ executive policy reversal → ↑ policy credibility → ↑ long-term investment; commitment device

Concept: The Malthusian trap operated historically because population growth always outstripped food production gains, keeping per capita income near subsistence until the industrial revolution broke the cycle.
Notes: Malthus trap: pop growth > food production → per capita income → subsistence level; Industrial Revolution → broke cycle via tech

Concept: As international law develops customary norms through consistent state practice plus opinio juris, it creates binding obligations even on states that did not explicitly consent.
Notes: customary intl law: consistent state practice + opinio juris → binding; ≠ treaty consent required; binds all states

Concept: Machiavelli separates political ethics from personal ethics, arguing that effective rulers must be willing to act immorally when state necessity requires, pioneering realpolitik.
Notes: Machiavelli: political ethics ≠ personal ethics; ruler must use immorality when necessary (ragione di stato); realpolitik

Concept: When the franchise was extended to previously excluded groups, political parties had to broaden their platforms, reshaping the political economy of redistribution and public goods provision.
Notes: ↑ franchise → ↑ electorate diversity → parties broaden platforms → ↑ redistribution + ↑ public goods provision; Meltzer-Richard

Concept: Hannah Arendt distinguishes labor (biological necessity), work (producing durable objects), and action (political participation among equals) as the three human activities in the vita activa.
Notes: Arendt vita activa: labor (biological necessity) ≠ work (durable objects) ≠ action (political participation among equals)

Concept: Increasing educational attainment of women correlates strongly with lower fertility rates, higher female labor force participation, and accelerated economic development.
Notes: ↑ female education → ↓ fertility + ↑ female labor participation → ↑ economic development; demographic transition

Concept: When states defect from international agreements for short-term gain, they damage their reputational capital, making future cooperation more costly because partners demand stronger enforcement mechanisms.
Notes: state defection from intl agreement → ↓ reputation → future cooperation ↑ costlier; partners demand ↑ enforcement; shadow of future

Concept: Polanyi's double movement: market expansion creates social dislocations that generate counter-movements demanding social protection, producing political tension between liberal and protective impulses.
Notes: Polanyi double movement: market expansion → social dislocation → protective counter-movement; liberal ↔ protective tension

Concept: As urbanization accelerates, agglomeration economies increase productivity through knowledge spillovers, labor market pooling, and intermediate input sharing, but also create congestion costs.
Notes: ↑ urbanization → agglomeration economies: ↑ knowledge spillovers + labor pooling + input sharing → ↑ productivity; ↑ congestion costs

Concept: When a constitutional court exercises judicial review to strike down legislation, it creates a counter-majoritarian tension with democratic principles but protects minority rights from majoritarian abuse.
Notes: constitutional court strikes down legislation → counter-majoritarian tension ↔ democratic principle; protects minority from majority; Dahl debate

Concept: Aristotle's eudaimonia is not merely pleasure or wealth but flourishing through virtuous activity in accordance with reason, requiring community, friendship, and adequate material conditions.
Notes: Aristotle eudaimonia: flourishing via virtuous rational activity ≠ pleasure/wealth; requires community + friendship + material adequacy

Concept: When countries coordinate on global public goods like climate change mitigation, free-rider incentives reduce contribution, necessitating side payments, sanctions, or repeated-game dynamics.
Notes: global public goods (climate): free-rider incentive → ↓ contribution; fix: side payments + sanctions + repeated game; coordination problem

Concept: The Iron Law of Oligarchy (Michels) states that all large organizations, even democratic ones, inevitably develop oligarchic leadership because coordination requires hierarchy.
Notes: Michels Iron Law: all large organizations → oligarchy; coordination needs hierarchy ≠ sustained democracy within orgs

Concept: Spinoza's pantheism identifies God with nature, implying that everything that exists is a mode of the single infinite substance, eliminating the mind-body dualism of Descartes.
Notes: Spinoza: God = nature (pantheism); everything = mode of single infinite substance; ∴ eliminates Cartesian mind-body dualism

Concept: When the number of veto players in a political system increases, the status quo becomes harder to change, producing more policy stability but also more difficulty in responding to crises.
Notes: ↑ veto players → ↓ policy change probability → ↑ stability; ↓ crisis response capacity; ∴ trade-off stability ↔ adaptability

Concept: Mill's harm principle holds that the only legitimate reason to restrict individual liberty is to prevent harm to others, not to protect individuals from themselves or enforce morality.
Notes: Mill harm principle: restrict liberty only to prevent harm to others ≠ self-harm ≠ enforce morality; individual sovereignty

Concept: As global supply chains lengthen and become more interconnected, a disruption in one node propagates downstream, increasing systemic fragility even while reducing average production costs.
Notes: ↑ supply chain length + interconnectedness → ↓ avg costs; disruption in 1 node → ↑ downstream propagation → ↑ systemic fragility

Concept: Utilitarianism fails to account for the separateness of persons because it allows the sacrifice of one person's welfare for aggregate benefit, which Rawls used to motivate his alternative.
Notes: utilitarianism: allows sacrifice of individual for aggregate → ignores separateness of persons; Rawls → difference principle as fix

Concept: When democratic backsliding occurs incrementally, each individual step seems insufficient to trigger strong responses, allowing cumulative erosion of institutions to proceed unchecked.
Notes: incremental democratic backsliding → each step ↓ trigger response threshold → cumulative erosion; Levitsky/Ziblatt 'how democracies die'

Concept: The diffusion of nuclear technology to non-state actors represents a qualitative shift in proliferation risk because deterrence requires rational and identifiable adversaries.
Notes: nuclear tech → non-state actors → deterrence fails; deterrence requires rational + identifiable adversary ≠ non-state; ↑ risk

Concept: As the middle class grows in developing countries, demand for democratic accountability and rule of law increases, supporting the modernization theory link between development and democracy.
Notes: ↑ middle class (developing countries) → ↑ demand for accountability + rule of law → ↑ democratization; modernization theory

Concept: Constructivism in international relations argues that state interests and identities are not fixed but are socially constructed through interaction, meaning cooperation can reshape interests over time.
Notes: constructivism IR: state interests + identities ≠ fixed; socially constructed through interaction; ∴ cooperation → reshapes interests over time

Concept: When Enlightenment thinkers applied reason to political authority, they delegitimized divine right monarchy and created intellectual foundations for popular sovereignty and constitutional government.
Notes: Enlightenment reason → delegitimized divine right → ↑ popular sovereignty + constitutional govt; Locke/Montesquieu/Rousseau

Concept: The combination of geographic isolation, unique resource endowments, and institutional path dependence explains why similar starting conditions can produce divergent long-run development outcomes.
Notes: geography + resource endowments + institutional path dependence → divergent long-run development; ≠ similar starting conditions guarantee similar outcomes
